## [00:00 - 03:00]

What's up guys? Cole Gordon here and welcome back to the training. This training is called the pitching phase, a three-part system to crush your sales pitch every time. So there's three phases to the pitch codex. There's transition, pitch, and close, or the committing phase. And then within each phase, there's different parts. For the pitching phase, there's three parts, and that's really what we're going to cover in this video. So just a brief overview of what we're covering. We're going to review just what the pitch codex is and why we pitch this way, as well as what the three parts are and the purpose of each part. So there's the high-level promise, the bridge, which are your pillars, and the delivery, and then how to construct each part for your offer specifically. So this would be probably a little bit of a long training, so let's get right into it. Just as a reminder, the pitch codex is basically a way to pitch your products and services that was inspired by the information marketing space. So why do we even bother doing that opposed to just what Brian Tracy teaches out of his book, the traditional methods? And really, I did a long explanation of this in the pitch codex overview video, which was the first video in week two. But to summarize, since 2008, trust is at an all-time low, and not just in high ticket, but in all markets. And at the same time, information, options, and competition is at an all-time high. So what this means is there's an added importance to the offer. This is the typo right here. It should be importance, but added importance to the offer. So as the buyer gets more control over the process and trust is lowered, really what's going to happen is the best offer is going to win. So what we want to do is have the best offer and be the best salespeople. We can't know. I mean, we can, but it's not ideal to just be the best salespeople with a crappy offer. We want to have both. And this is why when I was selling full-time, I really made it a point to try to find the best offer I could. And that served me really well. I made a lot of money doing that. But we want to be the best salespeople with the best offer. So if you're an entrepreneur, this means you have total control over this. So you can just actually control what offer you're selling and how you're pitching it. And really specific with the offer here, you want it to have new elements that, one, separates from the competition, and two, justify why their past attempts to succeed didn't work. This is so important. I've probably said it like a million times so far. But the ultimate pitch is one that, as you pitch it, as you explain it, it positions you as unique difference superior than everybody else. And at the same time, simultaneously, helps give the prospect insight to why everything they've tried in the past didn't work.

So the high level promise is basically one sentence, maybe two sentences, that states the end promise result that your offer is designed to provide. So we do this for a few reasons.
